{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

runContext.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
runContext.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
org_config_snapshot.resource_prompt:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI -- One-Shot Article Content Generation Spec (React Router TSX) -- v2026-04-26 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent (as a "system" / "developer" prompt) so the agent can generate brand-consistent MLAI article content modules in one shot for this repository. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Content should be practical, inclusive, and factual. Avoid hype and guarantees. Output format: TypeScript React component file (`.tsx`) for article body content. Framework: React Router 7 + React 19 + Tailwind. Import style: alias-first with `~/...` paths. --- ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given article inputs (slug/category/topic/body sections/FAQ/references/CTA text), generate a production-ready article content TSX file that compiles **without manual edits**. Framework constraints (required): - Use `react-router` APIs (e.g., `Link` with `to` prop). - Do **not** use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `use client`, metadata exports, etc.). - Do **not** use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use Tailwind utility classes consistent with this repo. Architecture constraints (required): - **NO `ArticleLayout` in content files**. Route handler wraps content already. - Content component must return `<>...` or `
...
`. - If using wrapper div, use `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Use design system components when available: - Persona cards → `AudienceGrid` (`variant`: `orange | purple | yellow` only) - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` (`variant`: `purple | orange` only) - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` (no raw `` blocks for article body sections) - Never use `placehold.co` URLs. Use `` or empty string placeholders. - Ensure `seo_config` path matches registry slug path exactly. Deliverable: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` (If requested, also provide companion updates for registry/SEO entries with matching slug/path.) --- ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: - `title`: string - `slug`: string - `category`: string (folder under `app/articles/content/`) - `summary`: string - `sections`: ordered list of body sections - `audienceCards` (optional): for "Who is this for?" - `quoteBlocks` (optional) - `images` (optional) - `faqItems` (optional) - `references` (optional) - `cta` (optional, generic wording only) Use placeholders exactly as provided (e.g., `{{TOPIC}}`). --- ## 2) Framework, file, and styling configuration - Language: TypeScript (`.tsx`) - Component pattern: `export default function ...() { ... }` - Routing library: `react-router` - Styling: Tailwind classes already used in repo - Typography plugin enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`) - Internal imports should prefer `~/...` aliases Common class patterns in this codebase: - Layout/spacing: `my-8`, `my-12`, `mt-8`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-3xl`, `p-6`, `sm:p-8` - Text colors: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600`, `text-white` - Borders/surfaces: `border border-gray-300`, `border-gray-400`, `bg-transparent` - Prose-safe wrappers for custom UI blocks: `not-prose` --- ## 3) Import rules (use real project paths) Use only imports that exist in this codebase. Typical imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Do not invent components/props. --- ## 4) Article content structure (no duplicate page header) Important: layout/hero/breadcrumb/title are handled upstream. So inside content file: - Start directly with body content (`h2`, `p`, lists, blocks). - Do **not** render duplicate title/breadcrumb/author header. - Keep heading hierarchy valid (`h2` for top-level sections, `h3` nested). Recommended section flow: 1. Intro paragraph(s) 2. Main sections (`h2`) 3. Optional persona block (`AudienceGrid`) 4. Optional quote block (`QuoteBlock`) 5. Optional image block (`ArticleImageBlock`) 6. FAQ (`ArticleFAQ`) 7. Generic CTA 8. Footer nav (`ArticleFooterNav`) --- ## 5) Design system component usage (strict) ### 5.1 Audience cards Use `AudienceGrid` for "Who is this for?" sections: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants only: `orange | purple | yellow`. ### 5.2 Quotes/callouts Use `QuoteBlock` (not ad-hoc bordered divs): ```tsx Keep your first AI implementation narrow, measurable, and reviewable. ``` Allowed variants only: `purple | orange`. ### 5.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`: ```tsx ``` No placeholder services (e.g., no `placehold.co`). --- ## 6) Links, references, and navigation - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` with `to`. - External links: `...`. - Use authoritative references when included. - Footer navigation can use: ```tsx ``` --- ## 7) CTA rules - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - ✅ Example: `Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}` - ❌ Do not use product-specific or service-specific names unless explicitly provided. Example with existing component: ```tsx ``` --- ## 8) Content quality and tone rules - Australian context where relevant. - Practical, factual, no guarantees. - No medical/legal-style warning blocks unless explicitly requested. - No service chips/tags sections. - Keep paragraphs concise and scannable. - Prefer concrete steps and examples. --- ## 9) Build-safety rules (one-shot accuracy) - File must compile with strict TypeScript. - Use valid, existing imports only. - Do not add unsupported props to components. - Keep variant strings within known unions. - Avoid fragile breadcrumb/icon patterns in content files. - Keep slug/path naming consistent across file path and SEO/registry references. --- ## 10) Brand QA checklist (must pass) - Uses repository design components for cards/callouts/images. - No duplicate hero/title section in content body. - Wrapper uses `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Tailwind classes align with existing style language. - CTA text remains generic with placeholders preserved. - No invented components. --- ## 11) Technical QA checklist (must pass) - React Router imports only (`react-router`). - No Next.js/Vue/Svelte APIs. - `.tsx` syntax valid. - All JSX tags closed and typed. - Internal links use `to`, external links use `href`. - No broken alias paths (`~/...`). --- ## 12) Critical do/don't summary (hard guardrails) DO - Generate `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx`. - Export a default function component. - Use `AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock` where applicable. - Use only allowed variants. - Preserve placeholders like `{{TOPIC}}`. - Keep content body-only (no layout wrapper). NEVER - Import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Use `bg-white` wrapper backgrounds. - Use unsupported variants (`blue`, `brand` for these DS blocks). - Use `placehold.co`. - Invent imports, components, or props. - Add duplicate page title/breadcrumb header inside content. --- ## 13) Suggested content primitives (repo-aligned) - Section heading: `

...

` - Body copy: `

...

` - Persona block: `AudienceGrid` - Quote/callout: `QuoteBlock` - Image: `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ: `ArticleFAQ` with `ArticleFAQItem[]` - CTA: `ArticleCompanyCTA` - Footer nav: `ArticleFooterNav` --- ## 14) Example scaffold (copy/paste starter) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the first step?', answer: 'Define one clear outcome for {{TOPIC}}.' }, ] export default function ArticleContent() { return (

This guide explains practical ways to approach {{TOPIC}}.

Who is this for?

Key insight

Start narrow, track results, and iterate.
) } ``` --- ## 15) How to use this prompt 1. Parse inputs (`{{TOPIC}}`, slug, category, sections, FAQ, CTA). 2. Generate a single `.tsx` article content module at: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` 3. Use existing repository components and exact import paths. 4. Keep output body-only (no `ArticleLayout` / duplicate hero). 5. Validate TypeScript + React Router compatibility. 6. Ensure slug/path consistency for any SEO/registry references requested. End of master spec (React Router TSX, MLAI codebase-aligned). ```
org_config_snapshot.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

tech_stack.framework_prompt_context: DETECTED FRAMEWORK: React Router RENDERING LIBRARY: react 19.2.1 ROUTING: react-router-7 (import from 'react-router', links use 'to' prop) STYLING: tailwind LANGUAGE: TypeScript COMPONENT FILE EXTENSION: .tsx BUILD TOOL: vite CRITICAL RULES FOR CODE GENERATION: - Do NOT use next/link, next/image, next/dynamic — these are Next.js-only packages - Do NOT use 'use client' or 'use server' directives — these are Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use getStaticProps, getServerSideProps, generateStaticParams — Next.js only - Do NOT use export const metadata — Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use next/font — Next.js only - Do NOT use Vue SFC (.vue files), Composition API, or Options API — not a Vue project - Do NOT use Svelte syntax (.svelte files), $: reactivity, or Svelte stores USE THESE PATTERNS INSTEAD: - For links: import from 'react-router' and use the 'to' prop - For images: use standard HTML tags - For styling: use plain CSS or the project's detected styling approach

pillars.name: AI Community Events and Meetups in Australia
pillars.description: This pillar focuses on discovering, choosing, and getting value from AI meetups, workshops, lightning talks, and community events across Australia. It directly supports MLAI's events programming for people who want practical learning, relevant peers, and a clearer entry point into the local AI community.
pillars.content_angle: A practical, community-first guide to finding real AI connections and hands-on learning through Australian events rather than generic networking.

pillars.name: AI Hackathons and Real-World Challenge Programs
pillars.description: This pillar covers hackathons, challenge-based programs, team formation, prototyping, and applied AI problem-solving. It aligns closely with MLAI's hackathons and event programs, helping builders, students, founders, and domain experts turn ideas into tangible projects.
pillars.content_angle: Show how hackathons can be practical pathways to collaboration, prototypes, and startup momentum, not just short-term coding competitions.

pillars.name: Practical AI Learning and Getting Started
pillars.description: This pillar helps beginners and early-stage practitioners learn AI and machine learning through accessible, community-based pathways. It supports MLAI's educational content, workshops, and events by answering common learning questions with practical next steps and applied examples.
pillars.content_angle: Lower the barrier to entry by pairing plain-English education with hands-on community participation instead of theory-heavy content alone.

pillars.name: AI Founder Support, Pitching and Investor Readiness
pillars.description: This pillar addresses how early-stage AI founders can build momentum, sharpen their pitch, create better updates, and start investor relationship-building earlier. It maps to MLAI's founder-oriented tools, practical guides, and community programs that help entrepreneurs move from idea to credible signal.
pillars.content_angle: Focus on founder communication and investor readiness as practical habits supported by community, feedback, and early visibility.
seed_keywords: how to pitch an ai startup

pillars.name: Cross-Disciplinary AI Collaboration
pillars.description: This pillar explores how technical builders, clinicians, researchers, and other domain experts can work together on real-world AI challenges. It reflects MLAI's community and hackathon model, where useful outcomes depend on better collaboration between technical and non-technical participants.
pillars.content_angle: Bridge the gap between builders and domain experts with practical guidance for team formation, problem definition, and applied outcomes.
seed_keywords: technical and non technical collaboration ai

pillars.name: Community Participation and Contributor Recognition
pillars.description: This pillar explains how members can contribute meaningfully inside a community, build credibility, and earn recognition through participation. It directly connects to MLAI's Slack community and Roo Points system, giving contributors a clearer path to involvement, visibility, and useful perks.
pillars.content_angle: Position contribution as a practical way to build relationships and credibility in an AI community, without framing it as a guaranteed pathway to jobs or compensation.
seed_keywords: how to contribute to a tech community

pillar_strategy.rationale: These pillars were chosen because they map directly to MLAI's strongest audience needs and core offerings: events, hackathons, practical learning, founder support, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and community participation. Together they cover the main discovery journeys of learners, builders, founders, contributors, and domain experts while staying grounded in MLAI's role as a practical, community-first home base for applied AI in Australia.

{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

spec.title: How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne
spec.meta_description: accelerator program melbourne guide for AI founders

summary_items.label: What's the best accelerator program for my startup?
summary_items.description: The best fit depends on your stage, eligibility, founder goals and current support needs. Compare programs by practical support, community relevance, timing and whether they help you keep building.

summary_items.label: What does an accelerator program do?
summary_items.description: An accelerator usually helps early-stage founders move faster through structure, feedback, mentorship, networks and resources. Program models vary, so founders should check the offer before applying.

summary_items.label: Do accelerators give funding?
summary_items.description: Some accelerators may offer funding, but terms, equity arrangements and eligibility differ by provider. MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, with application timing varying by round.

summary_question_candidates.question: What's the best accelerator program for my startup?
What does an accelerator program do?
How can I get funding for my startup?

faq_items.question: How should Melbourne founders compare accelerator options?
faq_items.answer: Compare stage fit, eligibility, support offered and timing. Look at whether the program matches your sector, founder background, university affiliation, social impact focus or need for mentorship, funding, networks and accountability.

faq_items.question: Is MAP the only accelerator option in Melbourne?
faq_items.answer: No. MAP is a prominent University of Melbourne-backed example, but Melbourne’s ecosystem also includes other accelerators, incubators, startup programs, investors and mission-led pathways such as Catalysr.

faq_items.question: What should AI founders look for beyond capital?
faq_items.answer: AI founders should look for technical peers, mentor access, events, hackathons, founder tools, community support and accountability. These can help maintain momentum between formal program milestones.

faq_items.question: Should every founder apply to an accelerator immediately?
faq_items.answer: Not always. Some founders may need to validate an idea, build a prototype, meet collaborators or join community programs before applying to a formal accelerator.

sections.heading: Why Melbourne founders search for accelerators
paragraphs: Searching for an accelerator program Melbourne founders can trust is usually not about finding a single list of names. Melbourne has a visible startup support landscape that includes accelerators, incubators, venture capital investors and university-backed entrepreneurship programs. The Melbourne Accelerator Program, for example, began as a startup accelerator and has grown into a University of Melbourne-backed centre for entrepreneurship that supports founders across different stages of the startup journey.

For AI and machine learning founders, the harder question is fit. A useful program should offer more than a deadline or application form. Founders often need accountability, lived experience from other builders, practical networks, funding pathways and support when the work gets difficult. This guide is designed to help you assess those needs before you apply, so you can choose a program that matches your stage, goals and execution needs.

sections.heading: What an accelerator program usually does
paragraphs: An accelerator program is usually built to help early-stage founders move faster with more structure. The common promise is not just learning content. It is compressed feedback, clearer priorities, mentor access, and a network that can help a team test whether its venture has a real path forward.

Melbourne Accelerator Program is a useful local example because it began in 2012 as a startup accelerator and is now described as a centre of entrepreneurship supporting founders across stages of the startup journey. Its public materials point to practical support such as accountability, lived experience, mentorship, funding, and startup resources. That mix is often what founders should look for in an accelerator program in Melbourne: people who have been close to the work, a reason to keep momentum, and access to relevant networks.

The details still vary by provider. Some programs may focus on a specific founder group, sector, university connection, or social impact goal. Funding, equity terms, application timing, and eligibility can also differ. A founder should treat the word accelerator as a signal to investigate the model, not as proof that every program offers the same deal.

callout.title: Founder check
callout.body: Before applying, confirm what the program actually provides: mentorship, funding terms, eligibility, time commitment, network access, and the kind of accountability you will receive.

sections.heading: The Melbourne accelerator landscape is broader than one program
paragraphs: A search for an accelerator program in Melbourne will quickly surface well-known names, but the right choice is not always the most visible one. The Melbourne Accelerator Program, or MAP, is a strong local example. It was originally founded in 2012 as a startup accelerator and is now described as a centre of entrepreneurship powered by the University of Melbourne. Its public profile highlights support for founders at different stages, with a community of founders and networks around them.

That does not mean every founder should treat MAP as the default path. Catalysr shows how different programs can be built around a specific founder community and mission. It describes itself as an Australian startup accelerator for high-performing migrant and refugee entrepreneurs, helping them build startups or social enterprises. Program directories also list Melbourne accelerators, incubators, startup programs and investors side by side, which reinforces a simple point: Melbourne support comes in several forms, not just one accelerator brand.

subsections.heading: Compare fit before reputation
paragraphs: Founders should compare programs by stage, eligibility, sector focus, community relevance and practical support. A university-linked program, a mission-led accelerator and a broader investor or incubator pathway may all help, but they serve different needs. The best accelerator program Melbourne founders can choose is the one that matches the company’s current stage and the founder’s real support needs.

sections.heading: How to assess whether a program fits your startup
paragraphs: Before you apply to an accelerator program in Melbourne, check whether the program is built for founders like you. The Melbourne Accelerator Program has been described as supporting early-stage startups with a University of Melbourne affiliation or a social impact focus. Catalysr focuses on migrant and refugee entrepreneurs building startups or social enterprises in Australia.

MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, but its 2025 round is listed as closed, with 2026 applications expected early in 2026. Catalysr highlights intensive entrepreneurship programs and a community of professionals, advisors and mentors. It is a program you can enter, use well and stay accountable to.

subsections.heading: A four-part fit check
paragraphs: First, check stage fit: is the program aimed at early-stage founders, founders at all stages, or a narrower group? Second, check eligibility fit: do you meet any affiliation, social impact, migrant or refugee founder, or other entry criteria? Third, check support fit: does the program offer the funding, mentorship, resources, networks or founder community you actually need? Fourth, check momentum fit: are applications open soon enough, and can you commit attention when the program runs?

sections.heading: What AI founders should look for beyond funding
paragraphs: For AI and machine learning founders, funding is only one part of the accelerator decision. Melbourne has programs, investors, incubators and venture capital options that list offers, deadlines and funding. That matters, but the stronger question is whether a program helps you keep building when the application round, pitch night or formal milestone is over.

MAP describes itself as having evolved from a startup accelerator into a broader centre of entrepreneurship that supports founders at all stages. Its public profile points to community, networks, accountability and lived founder experience as part of that support. Those signals are useful for AI founders because technical startups often need more than capital.

A broad entrepreneurship program can still be valuable for a technical team. It may help with founder discipline, market clarity, networks and exposure to the local ecosystem. But AI builders should also look for a community layer around the program. This is where events, hackathons, technical peers, Slack-based support, learning resources and practical founder tools can help between formal accelerator sessions.

Not every founder needs an accelerator immediately. For MLAI’s audience, the practical path is to match the next step to the real bottleneck: capital if capital is blocking progress, mentorship if the team needs direction, technical peers if the product needs sharper thinking, and community if momentum is the main risk.

sections.heading: Choose the next step that keeps you building
paragraphs: The best accelerator program in Melbourne is not the same for every founder. It depends on your startup stage, eligibility, goals and the support you need now. MAP, for example, describes its work as supporting founders at all stages of the startup journey, while current program listings also point founders toward deadlines, offers and funding details across Melbourne programs.
That outcome might be mentorship, funding, accountability, a stronger network or a clearer path to growth.

If you are building in AI, machine learning or technology, do not pause momentum while you wait for the right round to open. Use founder communities, events and practical resources to keep learning, meet collaborators and sharpen your plan.

cta.title: Keep building while you compare programs
cta.body: Join MLAI to meet AI builders, attend events and hackathons, use founder resources and stay connected while you prepare for the right accelerator pathway.

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "how-to-choose-an-accelerator-program-in-melbourne"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-11"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-11"
export const DESCRIPTION = "accelerator program melbourne guide for AI founders"
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-68c8552d-1225-4cf4-8330-3456ec5d8866.jpg?alt=media&token=6b48edb0-ce94-4df0-b8ba-c4d79bfc080d"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "AI founders discussing a Melbourne accelerator program over laptops in a candid close-up workspace scene"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "startups"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "How should Melbourne founders compare accelerator options?", answer: "Compare stage fit, eligibility, support offered and timing. Look at whether the program matches your sector, founder background, university affiliation, social impact focus or need for mentorship, funding, networks and accountability." },
  { id: 2, question: "Is MAP the only accelerator option in Melbourne?", answer: "No. MAP is a prominent University of Melbourne-backed example, but Melbourne\u2019s ecosystem also includes other accelerators, incubators, startup programs, investors and mission-led pathways such as Catalysr." },
  { id: 3, question: "What should AI founders look for beyond capital?", answer: "AI founders should look for technical peers, mentor access, events, hackathons, founder tools, community support and accountability. These can help maintain momentum between formal program milestones." },
  { id: 4, question: "Should every founder apply to an accelerator immediately?", answer: "Not always. Some founders may need to validate an idea, build a prototype, meet collaborators or join community programs before applying to a formal accelerator." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne",
  intro: "accelerator program melbourne guide for AI founders",
  items: [
    { label: "What's the best accelerator program for my startup?", description: "The best fit depends on your stage, eligibility, founder goals and current support needs. Compare programs by practical support, community relevance, timing and whether they help you keep building." },
    { label: "What does an accelerator program do?", description: "An accelerator usually helps early-stage founders move faster through structure, feedback, mentorship, networks and resources. Program models vary, so founders should check the offer before applying." },
    { label: "Do accelerators give funding?", description: "Some accelerators may offer funding, but terms, equity arrangements and eligibility differ by provider. MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, with application timing varying by round." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

  { question: "What does an accelerator program do?", answer: "An accelerator usually helps early-stage founders move faster through structure, feedback, mentorship, networks and resources. Program models vary, so founders should check the offer before applying." },
  { question: "Do accelerators give funding?", answer: "Some accelerators may offer funding, but terms, equity arrangements and eligibility differ by provider. MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, with application timing varying by round." },
  { question: "How should Melbourne founders compare accelerator options?", answer: "Compare stage fit, eligibility, support offered and timing. Look at whether the program matches your sector, founder background, university affiliation, social impact focus or need for mentorship, funding, networks and accountability." },
  { question: "Is MAP the only accelerator option in Melbourne?", answer: "No. MAP is a prominent University of Melbourne-backed example, but Melbourne\u2019s ecosystem also includes other accelerators, incubators, startup programs, investors and mission-led pathways such as Catalysr." },
  { question: "What should AI founders look for beyond capital?", answer: "AI founders should look for technical peers, mentor access, events, hackathons, founder tools, community support and accountability. These can help maintain momentum between formal program milestones." },
  { question: "Should every founder apply to an accelerator immediately?", answer: "Not always. Some founders may need to validate an idea, build a prototype, meet collaborators or join community programs before applying to a formal accelerator." },
]

        },
      })),
    })
  : null

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window === 'undefined') return
    if (!new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).has('cfInspector')) return
    const script = document.createElement('script')
    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

export default function ArticleContent() {
  const authorDetails = {
    name: AUTHOR,
    role: AUTHOR_ROLE,
    bio: AUTHOR_BIO,
    avatarUrl: AUTHOR_AVATAR,
  }

  return (
    <>
      {faqStructuredData ? (
        <script type="application/ld+json" dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: faqStructuredData }} />
      ) : null}
      <ContentFactoryInspectorBridge />
      <ArticleHeroHeader
        breadcrumbs={[
          { label: 'Home', href: '/', icon: Home },
          { label: 'Articles', href: "/articles" },
          { label: TOPIC, current: true },
        ]}
        title={TOPIC}
        titleHighlight={TOPIC}
        headerBgColor="cyan"
        summary={summaryHighlights}
        heroImage={HERO_IMAGE}
        heroImageAlt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
      />

      <ArticleTocPlaceholder className="bg-transparent" />

        <div id="section-01" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-01"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Why Melbourne founders search for accelerators"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-01"}>
        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"Searching for an accelerator program Melbourne founders can trust is usually not about finding a single list of names. Melbourne has a visible startup support landscape that includes accelerators, incubators, venture capital investors and university-backed entrepreneurship programs. The Melbourne Accelerator Program, for example, began as a startup accelerator and has grown into a University of Melbourne-backed centre for entrepreneurship that supports founders across different stages of the startup journey."}</p>
        <p>{"For AI and machine learning founders, the harder question is fit. A useful program should offer more than a deadline or application form. Founders often need accountability, lived experience from other builders, practical networks, funding pathways and support when the work gets difficult. This guide is designed to help you assess those needs before you apply, so you can choose a program that matches your stage, goals and execution needs."}</p>
        <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-01"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Hero image"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-01"}>
        <ArticleImageBlock
          src={HERO_IMAGE}
          alt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
          caption="accelerator program melbourne guide for AI founders"
          width={1600}
          height={1067}
        />
        </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-type={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-label={"Who is this guide for?"}>
          <AudienceGrid
            heading="Who is this guide for?"
            cards={[
            {
              title: 'Founders & Builders',
              description: 'For operators validating demand, pitching a vision, and moving before momentum stalls.',
              icon: <RocketLaunchIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'orange',
            },
            {
              title: 'Students & Switchers',
              description: 'For readers learning how strong technical partners evaluate traction, skills, and fit.',
              icon: <AcademicCapIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'purple',
            },
            {
              title: 'Community Builders',
              description: 'For connectors, mentors, and organisers helping founders meet collaborators in the right rooms.',
              icon: <UsersIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'yellow',
            },
            ]}
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:key-insight"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Key insight"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Key insight" variant="purple">
            {"The best fit depends on your stage, eligibility, founder goals and current support needs. Compare programs by practical support, community relevance, timing and whether they help you keep building."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>
        <div id="section-02" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What an accelerator program usually does"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <h2>{"What an accelerator program usually does"}</h2>
          <p>{"An accelerator program is usually built to help early-stage founders move faster with more structure. The common promise is not just learning content. It is compressed feedback, clearer priorities, mentor access, and a network that can help a team test whether its venture has a real path forward."}</p>
          <p>{"Melbourne Accelerator Program is a useful local example because it began in 2012 as a startup accelerator and is now described as a centre of entrepreneurship supporting founders across stages of the startup journey. Its public materials point to practical support such as accountability, lived experience, mentorship, funding, and startup resources. That mix is often what founders should look for in an accelerator program in Melbourne: people who have been close to the work, a reason to keep momentum, and access to relevant networks."}</p>
          <p>{"The details still vary by provider. Some programs may focus on a specific founder group, sector, university connection, or social impact goal. Funding, equity terms, application timing, and eligibility can also differ. A founder should treat the word accelerator as a signal to investigate the model, not as proof that every program offers the same deal."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: What an accelerator program usually does"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-4b9b7bc6-5e23-41d8-a5d3-7ce1e984b079.jpg?alt=media&token=0e260ed1-bbb4-40a0-aa6a-ca7789b2753b"
            alt="Founder notebook beside coffee and blurred mentor notes during an accelerator planning session"
            caption="What an accelerator program usually does"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <ArticleCallout title="Founder check" variant="brand">
            {"Before applying, confirm what the program actually provides: mentorship, funding terms, eligibility, time commitment, network access, and the kind of accountability you will receive."}
          </ArticleCallout>
        </div>
        <div id="section-03" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"The Melbourne accelerator landscape is broader than one program"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <h2>{"The Melbourne accelerator landscape is broader than one program"}</h2>
          <p>{"A search for an accelerator program in Melbourne will quickly surface well-known names, but the right choice is not always the most visible one. The Melbourne Accelerator Program, or MAP, is a strong local example. It was originally founded in 2012 as a startup accelerator and is now described as a centre of entrepreneurship powered by the University of Melbourne. Its public profile highlights support for founders at different stages, with a community of founders and networks around them."}</p>
          <p>{"That does not mean every founder should treat MAP as the default path. Catalysr shows how different programs can be built around a specific founder community and mission. It describes itself as an Australian startup accelerator for high-performing migrant and refugee entrepreneurs, helping them build startups or social enterprises. Program directories also list Melbourne accelerators, incubators, startup programs and investors side by side, which reinforces a simple point: Melbourne support comes in several forms, not just one accelerator brand."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: The Melbourne accelerator landscape is broader than one program"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-615f91b2-2a36-448a-9278-4acfc9f0c685.jpg?alt=media&token=06824a95-da34-4a01-be72-8cc73efbe1b6"
            alt="Candid view of a Melbourne startup workspace highlighting the city\u2019s broader accelerator scene"
            caption="The Melbourne accelerator landscape is broader than one program"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <h3>{"Compare fit before reputation"}</h3>
          <p>{"Founders should compare programs by stage, eligibility, sector focus, community relevance and practical support. A university-linked program, a mission-led accelerator and a broader investor or incubator pathway may all help, but they serve different needs. The best accelerator program Melbourne founders can choose is the one that matches the company\u2019s current stage and the founder\u2019s real support needs."}</p>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Get the checklist"}>
          <ArticleResourceCTA
            eyebrow="Free guide"
            title={"Get the accelerator program melbourne checklist"}
            description="Use this article as a working guide: shortlist candidates, validate traction, and structure your next conversations."
            buttonLabel="Download now"
            buttonHref="/articles"
            accent="purple"
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"step-list:practical-next-steps"} data-cf-component-type={"step-list"} data-cf-component-label={"Practical next steps"}>
          <ArticleStepList
            title="Practical next steps"
            steps={[
            "What an accelerator program usually does",
            "The Melbourne accelerator landscape is broader than one program",
            "How to assess whether a program fits your startup",
            "What AI founders should look for beyond funding",
            "Choose the next step that keeps you building",
            ]}
            accent="indigo"
          />
        </div>
        <div id="section-04" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-04"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"How to assess whether a program fits your startup"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-04"}>
          <h2>{"How to assess whether a program fits your startup"}</h2>
          <p>{"Before you apply to an accelerator program in Melbourne, check whether the program is built for founders like you. The Melbourne Accelerator Program has been described as supporting early-stage startups with a University of Melbourne affiliation or a social impact focus. Catalysr focuses on migrant and refugee entrepreneurs building startups or social enterprises in Australia."}</p>
          <p>{"MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, but its 2025 round is listed as closed, with 2026 applications expected early in 2026. Catalysr highlights intensive entrepreneurship programs and a community of professionals, advisors and mentors. It is a program you can enter, use well and stay accountable to."}</p>
          <h3>{"A four-part fit check"}</h3>
          <p>{"First, check stage fit: is the program aimed at early-stage founders, founders at all stages, or a narrower group? Second, check eligibility fit: do you meet any affiliation, social impact, migrant or refugee founder, or other entry criteria? Third, check support fit: does the program offer the funding, mentorship, resources, networks or founder community you actually need? Fourth, check momentum fit: are applications open soon enough, and can you commit attention when the program runs?"}</p>
        </div>
        <div id="section-05" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-05"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What AI founders should look for beyond funding"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-05"}>
          <h2>{"What AI founders should look for beyond funding"}</h2>
          <p>{"For AI and machine learning founders, funding is only one part of the accelerator decision. Melbourne has programs, investors, incubators and venture capital options that list offers, deadlines and funding. That matters, but the stronger question is whether a program helps you keep building when the application round, pitch night or formal milestone is over."}</p>
          <p>{"MAP describes itself as having evolved from a startup accelerator into a broader centre of entrepreneurship that supports founders at all stages. Its public profile points to community, networks, accountability and lived founder experience as part of that support. Those signals are useful for AI founders because technical startups often need more than capital."}</p>
          <p>{"A broad entrepreneurship program can still be valuable for a technical team. It may help with founder discipline, market clarity, networks and exposure to the local ecosystem. But AI builders should also look for a community layer around the program. This is where events, hackathons, technical peers, Slack-based support, learning resources and practical founder tools can help between formal accelerator sessions."}</p>
          <p>{"Not every founder needs an accelerator immediately. For MLAI\u2019s audience, the practical path is to match the next step to the real bottleneck: capital if capital is blocking progress, mentorship if the team needs direction, technical peers if the product needs sharper thinking, and community if momentum is the main risk."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-05"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: What AI founders should look for beyond funding"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-05"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-dd98f654-9c03-4c17-92e5-f823504cf619.jpg?alt=media&token=f144a404-fea5-46e4-8abf-2fb1ea137584"
            alt="AI founder\u2019s hands reviewing accelerator notes beside a laptop in a candid Melbourne workspace"
            caption="What AI founders should look for beyond funding"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>
        <div id="section-06" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-06"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Choose the next step that keeps you building"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-06"}>
          <h2>{"Choose the next step that keeps you building"}</h2>
          <p>{"The best accelerator program in Melbourne is not the same for every founder. It depends on your startup stage, eligibility, goals and the support you need now. MAP, for example, describes its work as supporting founders at all stages of the startup journey, while current program listings also point founders toward deadlines, offers and funding details across Melbourne programs."}</p>
          <p>{"That outcome might be mentorship, funding, accountability, a stronger network or a clearer path to growth."}</p>
          <p>{"If you are building in AI, machine learning or technology, do not pause momentum while you wait for the right round to open. Use founder communities, events and practical resources to keep learning, meet collaborators and sharpen your plan."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-06"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Choose the next step that keeps you building"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-06"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-feba4644-3ba2-45cb-a2c9-a601f4eb2cef.jpg?alt=media&token=90efb5c1-5dba-4002-ba9d-df82d9aff514"
            alt="Melbourne startup founders discussing next steps in a candid accelerator workshop scene"
            caption="Choose the next step that keeps you building"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:keep-moving-forward"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Keep moving forward"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Keep moving forward" variant="orange">
            {"Some accelerators may offer funding, but terms, equity arrangements and eligibility differ by provider. MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, with application timing varying by round."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"template-resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"template-resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Free MLAI Template Resource"}>
          <MLAITemplateResourceCTA />
        </div>

      <div data-cf-component-id={"references"} data-cf-component-type={"references"} data-cf-component-label={"Authoritative References"}>
        <ArticleReferences
          references={[
            {id: 1, href: "https://lightningventures.com.au/case-studies/melbourne-accelerator-program-case-study/", title: "How Melbourne Accelerator Program Case Study achieved results with Lightning Ventures | Lightning Ventures", publisher: "lightningventures.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting How Melbourne Accelerator Program Case Study achieved results with Lightning Ventures | Lightning Ventures.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 2, href: "https://incubatorlist.com/best-venture-capital-firms-in-melbourne", title: "Top VCs & Startup Programs in Melbourne (2026) \u2014 Open Deadlines & Funding | IncubatorList", publisher: "incubatorlist.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Top VCs & Startup Programs in Melbourne (2026) \u2014 Open Deadlines & Funding | IncubatorList.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 3, href: "https://au.linkedin.com/company/melbourne-accelerator-program", title: "Melbourne Accelerator Program (MAP) | LinkedIn", publisher: "au.linkedin.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Melbourne Accelerator Program (MAP) | LinkedIn.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 4, href: "https://www.themap.co/accelerator/startups", title: "Accelerator Startups \u2014 Melbourne Accelerator Program", publisher: "themap.co", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Accelerator Startups \u2014 Melbourne Accelerator Program.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 5, href: "https://www.meetventures.com/post/best-practices-for-running-a-successful-accelerator-program", title: "Best Practices for Running a Successful Accelerator Program", publisher: "meetventures.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Best Practices for Running a Successful Accelerator Program.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 6, href: "https://pattens.com/grants/melbourne-accelerator-program-map-vic/", title: "Melbourne Accelerator Program (MAP) VIC", publisher: "pattens.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Melbourne Accelerator Program (MAP) VIC.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 7, href: "https://mu.linkedin.com/posts/arowse_your-guide-to-apply-to-the-melbourne-accelerator-activity-7028524017175191552-e_Dm", title: "Your guide to apply to the Melbourne Accelerator Program | Andrew Rowse", publisher: "mu.linkedin.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Your guide to apply to the Melbourne Accelerator Program | Andrew Rowse.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 8, href: "https://www.melbconnect.com.au/community/melbourne-entrepreneurial-centre", title: "Melbourne Connect | Melbourne Entrepreneurial Centre", publisher: "melbconnect.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Melbourne Connect | Melbourne Entrepreneurial Centre.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 9, href: "https://catalysr.com.au/", title: "Catalysr", publisher: "catalysr.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Catalysr.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 10, href: "https://rosinger.com.au/startup-accelerators/", title: "Startup Accelerators & Startup Assistance - Studio Rosinger", publisher: "rosinger.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Startup Accelerators & Startup Assistance - Studio Rosinger.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 11, href: "https://launchvic.org/events-and-networking/", title: "Events & Networking | LaunchVic", publisher: "launchvic.org", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Events & Networking | LaunchVic.", category: "guide"},

import { useEffect } from 'react'
import type { ReactNode } from 'react'
import { Home } from 'lucide-react'
import { AcademicCapIcon, RocketLaunchIcon, UsersIcon } from '@heroicons/react/24/outline'

import { ArticleFAQ } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFAQ'

import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader'

import { ArticleFooterNav } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFooterNav'
import QuoteBlock from '../../../components/articles/QuoteBlock'
import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '../../../components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder'
import AudienceGrid from '../../../components/articles/AudienceGrid'
import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA'

import { ArticleCallout } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCallout'
import MLAITemplateResourceCTA from '../../../components/articles/MLAITemplateResourceCTA'
import { ArticleReferences } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleReferences'
import ArticleDisclaimer from '../../../components/articles/ArticleDisclaimer'

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "how-to-choose-an-accelerator-program-in-melbourne"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-11"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-11"
export const DESCRIPTION = "accelerator program melbourne guide for AI founders"
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-68c8552d-1225-4cf4-8330-3456ec5d8866.jpg?alt=media&token=6b48edb0-ce94-4df0-b8ba-c4d79bfc080d"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "AI founders discussing a Melbourne accelerator program over laptops in a candid close-up workspace scene"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "startups"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "How should Melbourne founders compare accelerator options?", answer: "Compare stage fit, eligibility, support offered and timing. Look at whether the program matches your sector, founder background, university affiliation, social impact focus or need for mentorship, funding, networks and accountability." },
  { id: 2, question: "Is MAP the only accelerator option in Melbourne?", answer: "No. MAP is a prominent University of Melbourne-backed example, but Melbourne\u2019s ecosystem also includes other accelerators, incubators, startup programs, investors and mission-led pathways such as Catalysr." },
  { id: 3, question: "What should AI founders look for beyond capital?", answer: "AI founders should look for technical peers, mentor access, events, hackathons, founder tools, community support and accountability. These can help maintain momentum between formal program milestones." },
  { id: 4, question: "Should every founder apply to an accelerator immediately?", answer: "Not always. Some founders may need to validate an idea, build a prototype, meet collaborators or join community programs before applying to a formal accelerator." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne",
  intro: "accelerator program melbourne guide for AI founders",
  items: [
    { label: "What's the best accelerator program for my startup?", description: "The best fit depends on your stage, eligibility, founder goals and current support needs. Compare programs by practical support, community relevance, timing and whether they help you keep building." },
    { label: "What does an accelerator program do?", description: "An accelerator usually helps early-stage founders move faster through structure, feedback, mentorship, networks and resources. Program models vary, so founders should check the offer before applying." },
    { label: "Do accelerators give funding?", description: "Some accelerators may offer funding, but terms, equity arrangements and eligibility differ by provider. MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, with application timing varying by round." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "How to choose an accelerator program in Melbourne",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "What's the best accelerator program for my startup?", answer: "The best fit depends on your stage, eligibility, founder goals and current support needs. Compare programs by practical support, community relevance, timing and whether they help you keep building." },
  { question: "What does an accelerator program do?", answer: "An accelerator usually helps early-stage founders move faster through structure, feedback, mentorship, networks and resources. Program models vary, so founders should check the offer before applying." },
  { question: "Do accelerators give funding?", answer: "Some accelerators may offer funding, but terms, equity arrangements and eligibility differ by provider. MAP is described as offering equity-free funding, mentorship and resources, with application timing varying by round." },
  { question: "How should Melbourne founders compare accelerator options?", answer: "Compare stage fit, eligibility, support offered and timing. Look at whether the program matches your sector, founder background, university affiliation, social impact focus or need for mentorship, funding, networks and accountability." },
  { question: "Is MAP the only accelerator option in Melbourne?", answer: "No. MAP is a prominent University of Melbourne-backed example, but Melbourne\u2019s ecosystem also includes other accelerators, incubators, startup programs, investors and mission-led pathways such as Catalysr." },
  { question: "What should AI founders look for beyond capital?", answer: "AI founders should look for technical peers, mentor access, events, hackathons, founder tools, community support and accountability. These can help maintain momentum between formal program milestones." },
  { question: "Should every founder apply to an accelerator immediately?", answer: "Not always. Some founders may need to validate an idea, build a prototype, meet collaborators or join community programs before applying to a formal accelerator." },
]

const faqStructuredData = faqSchemaItems.length
  ? JSON.stringify({
      '@context': 'https://schema.org',
      '@type': 'FAQPage',
      mainEntity: faqSchemaItems.map((item) => ({
        '@type': 'Question',
        name: item.question,
        acceptedAnswer: {
          '@type': 'Answer',
          text: item.answer,
        },
      })),
    })
  : null
